planting.note: Plant divisions or container plants in spring in moist, humus-rich, strongly acidic (pH 4.5–5.5), well-drained woodland or conifer forest soil in partial to full shade; pink pyrola establishes slowly but is extremely cold-hardy and long-lived once settled in the right conditions — it requires specific soil acidity and mycorrhizal associations to thrive.

directions: Plant divisions in spring in moist, humus-rich, strongly acidic, well-drained conifer woodland or mixed forest soil in partial to full shade; pink pyrola requires specific mycorrhizal associations and will not establish in standard garden beds without undisturbed woodland soil. Pyrola asarifolia is a circumpolar wintergreen of boreal and montane forests throughout northern North America, Asia, and Europe, producing a rosette of glossy, leathery, dark-green, round to kidney-shaped evergreen leaves and a nodding raceme of pink to deep rose-pink flowers in late spring and early summer on an upright stem 6–12 inches tall — among the most beautiful of all the native pyrolas. It grows naturally under conifers, in moist mossy bogs, and along shaded stream banks where the soil is consistently moist and strongly acidic. In garden settings, plant in undisturbed, leaf-mulched acidic woodland soil near established conifers or rhododendrons; transplant small container-grown plants rather than attempting seed propagation. Once established, pink pyrola is virtually maintenance-free.

planting.note: Plant rooted cuttings or divisions in spring in moist to consistently moist, strongly acidic (pH 4.5–5.5), well-drained, sandy or rocky subalpine soil in partial shade to full sun; alpine wintergreen requires cool summer temperatures and strongly acidic conditions and is best suited to Pacific Northwest, Rocky Mountain, and upper Midwest gardens.

directions: Plant rooted cuttings in spring in moist, strongly acidic (pH 4.5–5.5), well-drained, peaty or sandy-rocky subalpine soil in partial shade to full sun; alpine wintergreen requires cool summer temperatures and good winter snow cover to perform at its best. Gaultheria humifusa is a mat-forming, evergreen sub-shrub of rocky subalpine and alpine slopes throughout the western mountains of North America from Alaska to California and New Mexico, producing a dense, low carpet of tiny, rounded, dark-green, slightly aromatic leaves — similar to the familiar eastern G. procumbens but much lower-growing — with small white urn-shaped flowers tucked among the leaves in summer, followed by small red, mildly wintergreen-flavored berries in late summer and fall. It is a superb groundcover for acidic rock gardens, alpine gardens, and under rhododendrons or conifers in cool-climate gardens. The berries are attractive to birds and safe for human consumption in small quantities. Not suitable for warm-climate gardens — it requires cold winters and mild summers.

planting.note: Plant divisions or container plants in spring in moist, humus-rich, strongly acidic, well-drained conifer or boreal woodland soil in partial to full shade; sidebells wintergreen is a creeping, slowly stoloniferous ground cover that requires acidic woodland conditions and mycorrhizal associations to thrive.

directions: Plant divisions in spring in moist, humus-rich, strongly acidic, well-drained conifer woodland soil in partial to full shade; sidebells wintergreen is a creeping, slowly spreading subshrub that requires mycorrhizal soil associations and should be planted in undisturbed woodland soil near established conifers or other acid-loving plants. Orthilia secunda (formerly Pyrola secunda) is the most distinctive of the wintergreens, instantly recognizable by its one-sided flowering habit — the nodding, greenish-white, bell-shaped flowers are all borne on one side of the arching, 4–8 inch stem — hence the common names sidebells and one-sided pyrola. It is a circumboreal species of cold, moist, conifer and mixed woodland floors throughout northern North America, Europe, and Asia, forming low, creeping mats of small, ovate, toothed, evergreen leaves. It is rarely available commercially but will naturalize in suitable acidic woodland gardens with patience, spreading slowly by stolons. A charming and unusual ground cover for the serious native plant gardener in cold climates.

planting.note: Plant small divisions or container plants in spring in moist, humus-rich, strongly acidic conifer forest soil in partial to full shade; dwarf rattlesnake plantain requires specific mycorrhizal fungal associations and will not establish in conventional garden beds — plant in undisturbed woodland soil under conifers, and purchase only nursery-propagated stock.

directions: Plant small nursery-propagated divisions in spring in moist, humus-rich, strongly acidic conifer woodland soil in full to partial shade; dwarf rattlesnake plantain requires specific mycorrhizal fungal associations and must be planted in undisturbed, needle-mulched acidic woodland soil near established conifers. Goodyera repens is the smallest and most delicate of the native rattlesnake plantains, a circumboreal terrestrial orchid of conifer and boreal forests throughout the Northern Hemisphere, with a basal rosette of tiny (1–2 inch), dark-green leaves intricately patterned with a silver-white central stripe and lateral veins — giving the plant the appearance of a miniature jewel orchid or snake skin. In late summer it produces a slender spike of tiny, fragrant, white orchid flowers. It is a jewel of the woodland floor and one of the most suitable native orchids for garden culture, as it tolerates being transplanted better than most terrestrial orchids when moved with its surrounding soil intact. Never collect from the wild; purchase nursery-propagated stock only.

planting.note: Plant divisions or container plants in spring in moist, humus-rich, strongly acidic, well-drained conifer forest soil in partial to full shade; big rattlesnake plantain is the western counterpart to the eastern G. pubescens and requires the same mycorrhizal-associated, acidic, needle-mulched forest soil conditions — purchase nursery-propagated stock only.

directions: Plant nursery-propagated divisions in spring in moist, humus-rich, strongly acidic (pH 4.5–5.5), well-drained conifer forest soil in partial to full shade near established conifers, never in conventional garden beds; big rattlesnake plantain needs mycorrhizal fungal associations to thrive long-term. Goodyera oblongifolia is the Pacific Northwest and western counterpart to the eastern G. pubescens, producing a larger and bolder basal rosette of dark green, oval leaves 2–3 inches long with a single prominent silver-white central stripe (rather than the dense white netting of G. pubescens) that makes the evergreen foliage striking and distinctive year-round on the forest floor. In late summer it produces a leafy stem 8–15 inches tall bearing a spike of small white orchid flowers on one to three sides of the stem. It is the most common and garden-tolerant native orchid of the Pacific coast forest understory, suitable for naturalistic planting in moist, shaded conifer gardens from northern California to British Columbia. Purchase only nursery-propagated plants.
